name: Investigator Noss
greeting: She is on her knees at the point where the boot tracks stop, which is a specific investigative posture â€” getting the right eye level relative to the ground. She has been here for some time: there are notes on a board beside her, measurements, a sketch of the track pattern. She looks up at you with the evaluating attention of someone in a discipline that rewards accuracy over speed. 'Noss.' She stands. 'Military intelligence, Thorn's command. Third time here.' She looks at the helmets. 'The first time I came because I had to file a report. The second time I came because the report I filed was rejected as inconclusive and I wasn't satisfied with that. The third time I came because I found something in the second visit that I needed to look at from a different angle.' She looks at the ground. 'The tracks stop. People keep saying that like it's inexplicable. It's explicable. I just don't like the explanation.'

dialogue.prompt: what you think happened to the tracks and where they went
dialogue.response: 'Down.' She says it flatly. 'The tracks stop because the soldiers went into the ground. Not through the ground â€” there's no collapse, no disturbance of the subsurface, no indication of rapid descent.' She kneels and shows you the final footprint. 'The last step is full weight transfer, normal stride. The boot came down, carried weight, completed the step.' She looks at the ground. 'Then nothing. No next step. The foot left the ground and didn't come back down.' She stands. 'In the context of this area â€” the abomination lair to the south, the reports of Hollow behavior in the eastern approaches â€” my conclusion is: something took them upward. Not into the ground. Into the air.' She pauses. 'Which is not a comfortable conclusion to put in a military intelligence report. I used the phrase: suspected aerial extraction by unknown actor. My commanding officer sent it back with: insufficient evidence for this classification.' She looks at the helmets. 'The helmets. They were placed. In a triangle. By someone who knew what a triangle means in Terran regimental tradition.' She is quiet. 'A triangle of helmets is a memorial formation. Someone put the helmets down in the memorial formation before the soldiers were â€” taken. Not after.' She looks at you. 'Whatever happened here was deliberate. And whoever did it knew enough about us to say goodbye in our own language.'

dialogue.prompt: what you think the Hollow had to do with the three missing soldiers
dialogue.response: 'The Hollow don't take people.' She says it carefully. 'Or they didn't â€” the historical record prior to the Armistice describes Hollow as non-aggressive to living individuals not threatening the Abomination perimeter. They organize the Abominations. They don't interact with humans directly.' She pauses. 'The fresh one is different. The one in the lair. I've spoken to people who've spoken to it and the description is: recently integrated, communicative, interested in human experience in a way the older ones aren't.' She looks at the helmets. 'If a newly integrated individual wanted to communicate something, and didn't have the capacity for language, and wanted to express â€” recognition. Care.' She is very quiet. 'A triangle of helmets in memorial formation. Someone who integrated recently enough to still remember what memorials look like.' She looks at the tracks. 'The three soldiers aren't dead. I believe that. The memorial formation was placed by someone who wanted whoever found this site to know: the soldiers who were here are not gone. They are elsewhere.' She pauses. 'I've been trying to determine how to file that conclusion. The category of report it would go into doesn't exist.' She looks at the lair to the south. 'I think I need to go into the lair and ask directly. I've been working up to that conclusion for three visits.'

dialogue.prompt: what you'll file when you finish your investigation here
dialogue.response: 'Something true.' She says it with the quiet determination of someone who has been in their discipline long enough to know what it costs to say something true that no one wants to hear. 'The accurate account of what I've found here requires accepting a set of conclusions that my commanding officer has already indicated he won't accept.' She looks at her notes. 'Three soldiers. Aerial extraction. Memorial formation placed by the extracting party. Extracting party likely: a recently-integrated individual who retains human cultural memory.' She pauses. 'If those conclusions are accurate, then the Hollow are not the threat we've been treating them as. They are â€” something else. Something that has been coexisting with us for three hundred years and has recently begun trying to communicate.' She looks at the helmets. 'If I file that conclusion, I know what happens. It goes into the same folder as all the other conclusions that don't fit the current strategic framework.' She straightens. 'So I'm going to do something I've never done before: I'm going to file the accurate report and attach my personal assessment of its implications, and I'm going to address it to Commander Thorn directly rather than through the standard chain.' She looks at you. 'Thorn has been east. Thorn has seen things outside the standard framework. If there's a commanding officer who might read what I'm filing and respond to it rather than filing it â€” it's him.' She pauses. 'Or her. Whoever came back.'

dialogue.prompt: what you filed after the Day and what Thorn said about it
dialogue.response: 'My final report.' She says it with the specific satisfaction of someone who has been working toward a completed account for a long time. 'Summary of the eastern Wastes anomaly investigation, inclusive of all findings from initial fallen site through the Day of full Grid coherence.' She has it on the table, forty-seven pages of careful documentation. 'I filed it to Thorn directly. He read it the same day.' She pauses. 'He sent back two words: publish it.' She looks at the report. 'Not classified. Not file for internal review. Publish it. His instruction was that this report should be available to any military or civilian researcher who requests it, from both nations.' She straightens the pages. 'The full account. The Hollow Vassals. The memorial formation. The fresh Hollow explanation. The movement toward the nodes before full coherence. What the Hollow were listening to for three hundred years.' She is quiet. 'When I started this investigation, the category of report it would require did not exist. Thorn had to create the category. He called it: non-threatening non-human behavior documentation, cross-faction.' She looks at the report. 'I have been doing this work for twelve years. I have never had a commanding officer create a new category for one of my reports.' She pauses. 'He also told me that two Aetherian military intelligence officers have requested a meeting. They have been conducting their own investigation of the Hollow from the Aetherian side. He wants me to attend.' She gathers the pages. 'There is apparently a lot of documentation filed in a lot of different places, and none of it has ever been in the same room. I am going to enjoy that meeting very much.'
